text: sharing the case of a right colectomy for a patient with right colon polyposis and large cecal polyps that were found initially two years ago. Patient was counseled for surgery. by his gastroenterologist and another surgeon, but because he had multiple prior operations, including two X-lapsed through a Chevron incision for trauma, and one was a thoracal abdominal on the left side and an open common bile duct exploration on the right side, he was told he could only have an open operation. And believe it or not, challenged the patient to decide not even to have surgery at the time that the biopsies of the colon polyps were all benign. Cut to two years later, he has another colonoscopy and now the cecal polyp is a cancer.

The surgeon begins the procedure with a left parumbilical incision for camera placement using a 12mm balloon trocar, along with a right lower quadrant 5mm port and a suprapubic 12mm port. The surgeon is taking down omental adhesions from the anterior abdominal wall to locate the transverse colon, which is marked with a tattoo distal to the colonic polyposis. The camera is positioned in the suprapubic port, revealing small bowel adhesions in the left upper quadrant. The surgeon continues to remove omental adhesions, starting to visualize the colon.

The surgeon reduces the hernia through the right side of the Chevron incision, revealing diastatic changes in the anterior abdominal wall. Adhesions of the omentum are visible, and the transverse colon is plastered to the Chevron incision and liver bed. The surgeon attempts to locate the tattoo marking the distal extent of the right-sided colon polyposis but is unable to find it. The ileocolic pedicle is tented, and lateral mobilization is performed to improve exposure. The camera is positioned in the suprapubic port, with working hands in the left lower quadrant and left parambulical port, as the surgeon mobilizes the cecum using an inferior approach.

The surgeon continues dissection around the ileocolic pedicle, aiming to locate the duodenum and navigate around the hepatic flexure. The duodenum is visible as the surgeon works to mobilize the colon from the gallbladder fossa. The camera is positioned in the suprapubic port, with working instruments in the left lower quadrant and left parumbilical port. The surgeon burns tissue slightly while searching for the transverse colon and tattoo in the mesentery, attempting to detach the colon from the liver edge through lateral dissection.

The surgeon navigates around the hepatic flexure, joining the previous lateral dissection to expose the colon. The tattoo marking the distal margin is visible several centimeters distal to the last colon polyp on the right side. The surgeon works to free the colon from the liver edge and mobilizes further distally to prepare for the anastomosis.

The surgeon attempts to lift the ileocolic pedicle after completing lateral dissection, using sponges to hold surrounding tissues. A window is created around the ileocolic pedicle for further mobilization.

The surgeon identifies the pedicle in front of them and proceeds with the dissection, achieving a safe ligation and removing thirty-two lymph nodes, all of which are negative. The dissection continues through to the previous area, confirming the absence of a right colic artery. The surgeon targets the right branch of the middle colic artery and approaches the colon margin marked by the tattoo. The omentum is split at the distal margin, and the omentum is taken en bloc with the specimen.

The surgeon takes the small bowel mesentery and uses ICG while stapling the margins, with the stapler entering through the suprapubic port. The specimen is placed above the liver temporarily. An isoparastaltic intracorporeal anastomosis is prepared by making a colotomy and bringing in the stapler with a vascular load for the common channel. The lumen is open with no bleeding, and the common channel is sewn shut using two needle drivers, alternating between right and left hand suturing for optimal angles.

The surgeon switches back to right-handed suturing to reinforce the anastomosis along the cumular neurotomy using a 3-0 V-lock suture. Clips are applied to secure the sutures, and the omentum is used to cover the anastomosis. The specimen is prepared to be extracted through a fan and steel incision at the port site.
